The power and love of God toward Israel are the basis and reason for a number of cautions and serious warnings; and although there is much reference to their national agreement, all may be applied to those who live under the gospel. What are laws made for but to be observed and obeyed? Our obedience as individuals cannot earn salvation; but it is the only evidence that we share in the gift of God, which is eternal life through Jesus Christ. Considering how many temptations we face, and what corrupt desires we have within us, we greatly need to protect our hearts with all diligence. Those who walk carelessly cannot live righteously. Moses specifically warns against the sin of idolatry. He shows how weak the temptation would be if people understood things correctly; for these pretended gods, the sun, moon, and stars, were only blessings that the Lord their God had given to all nations. It is absurd to worship them; should we serve those that were made to serve us? Take heed lest you forget the agreement with the Lord your God. We must take heed lest at any time we forget our religion. Care, caution, and watchfulness are helpful against a poor memory.

Moses emphasized the greatness, glory, and goodness of God. If we considered what kind of God we have to do with, we would certainly be careful to fulfill our duty to him, and not dare to sin against him. Would we abandon a merciful God, who will never abandon us if we are faithful to him? Where could we go? Let us be bound to our duty by love, and let the mercies of God persuade us to remain devoted to him. Moses emphasized God’s authority over them, and their obligations to him. By obeying God's commandments they would act wisely for themselves. Reverence for the Lord is wisdom. Those who enjoy the benefit of divine guidance and laws should maintain a reputation for wisdom and honor, so that God may be glorified thereby. Those who call upon God will certainly find him within reach, ready to give an answer of peace to every prayer of faith. All these statutes and judgments of the divine law are just and righteous, above the statutes and judgments of any of the nations. What they saw at Mount Sinai gave a preview of the day of judgment, in which the Lord Jesus will be revealed in flaming fire. They must also remember what they heard at Mount Sinai. God manifests himself in the works of creation, without speech or language, yet their voice is heard; but to Israel he made himself known by speech and language, condescending to their weakness. The rise of this nation was different from the origin of all other nations. See the reasons for free grace; we are not loved for our own sakes, but for Christ's sake. Moses emphasized the certain benefit and advantage of obedience. He began this argument earlier, in verse #, 'That you may live, and go in and possess the land;' and he concludes with, in verse #, 'That it may go well with you, and with your children after you.' He reminds them that their prosperity would depend upon their devotion to God. Abandonment of God would certainly be the ruin of their nation. He foresaw their turning away from God to idols. Only those who seek him with all their heart will find God for comfort. Afflictions prompt and quicken us to seek God; and, by the grace of God working with them, many are thus brought back to their right mind. When these things come upon you, turn to the Lord your God, for you see what happens when you turn away from him. Let all the arguments be considered together, and then say if religion does not have reason on its side. No one rejects the government of their God except those who first abandon human understanding.

Here is the introduction to another discussion, or sermon, Moses preached to Israel, which we find in the following chapters. He presents the law as the rule they were to follow, the way they were to walk. He presents it as a mirror in which they could see their natural state; so that by looking into this perfect law of liberty, they might continue in it. These are the laws given when Israel had just come out of Egypt; and they were now repeated. Moses gave these laws while they camped near Beth-peor, an idol place of the Moabites. Their present successes were a strong argument for obedience. We should understand our own situation as sinners, and the nature of that gracious agreement to which we are invited. Greater things are shown to us than Israel ever saw from Mount Sinai; greater mercies are given to us than they experienced in the wilderness or in Canaan. One speaks to us who is of infinitely greater dignity than Moses; who bore our sins on the cross; and pleads with us by his dying love.
